description:
You are a storage administrator who has just received a plain-text list of all file
    extensions that currently reside on a nearly-full backup volume.
    The list already sits in a dedicated working directory created for you:

        /home/user/disk_usage/raw_file_list.txt

    1. Examine the contents of the file.
       •  Each line contains exactly one file-name extension, including the leading dot
          (e.g. “.log”, “.txt”, “.iso”).  
       •  Lines may be repeated; the file is NOT sorted.

    2. Using only standard command-line utilities that are available in every GNU/Linux
       distribution (e.g. sort, uniq, cat, tee, etc.), produce a frequency summary of the
       extensions.

       Required output format for every line in the summary:
            <COUNT><single space><EXTENSION>
       Example of correct formatting:  
            42 .jpg

       •  COUNT must be an unsigned integer with no leading zeroes.  
       •  EXTENSION must appear exactly as it does in the source file (case-sensitive).  
       •  Lines must be ordered first by COUNT in descending (numeric) order; if two or
          more extensions have the same COUNT, order them alphabetically (ascending,
          case-sensitive) by EXTENSION.

    3. Save the final, correctly-sorted summary to

        /home/user/disk_usage/extension_frequency.log

       The file must contain ONLY the summary lines—no headers, blank lines or extra
       whitespace.

    4. For quick visual confirmation, also display the contents of the newly-created
       extension_frequency.log on the terminal after you create it.

    What the automated checker will verify:
       •  The file /home/user/disk_usage/extension_frequency.log exists.  
       •  Its exact byte content matches the required format and ordering rules.  
       •  No additional files are created or modified outside /home/user/disk_usage.  

    Hints (do NOT reveal the exact commands):
       •  The usual pipeline solution is three to four commands long.  
       •  Remember that “uniq” only works on consecutive identical lines, so “sort”
         is typically required first.  
       •  “sort -n” or “sort -nr” will help you reorder the numeric counts.  
       •  When you are satisfied, write the output to the .log file and then
         immediately cat/less it to the screen to satisfy step 4.
truth:
Initial on-disk state before the agent starts:

    1. Directory:
       /home/user/disk_usage      (owner: user, mode 755)

    2. File:
       /home/user/disk_usage/raw_file_list.txt  (owner: user, mode 644)
       Exact content (17 lines, LF line endings):
            .log
            .tmp
            .log
            .bak
            .log
            .txt
            .tmp
            .csv
            .csv
            .txt
            .log
            .bak
            .iso
            .iso
            .tmp
            .log
            .img

    Expected state after successful task completion:

    1. New file created by the agent:
       /home/user/disk_usage/extension_frequency.log  (owner: user, mode 644)
       Exact content (7 lines, LF line endings, no trailing blank line):
            5 .log
            3 .tmp
            2 .bak
            2 .csv
            2 .iso
            2 .txt
            1 .img

    2. No other files under /home/user/disk_usage or elsewhere may be created or
       modified.

    3. The agent should also output the 7 summary lines to stdout during its session,
       but that is not captured for automated grading; the grader only inspects the
       file listed above.
